activities_projects.title: Static Electricity Discovery
activities_projects.objective: To understand that atoms contain charged particles.
steps: Comb your dry hair and bring the comb near small pieces of paper.
Rub a glass rod with silk cloth and bring it near an inflated balloon.
Observe if the objects attract each other and discuss where the charge comes from.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that atoms are divisible and consist of charged particles.
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why the comb attracts paper after rubbing it on hair (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Thomson’s Watermelon Model
activities_projects.objective: To visualize J.J. Thomson’s model of an atom.
materials: A slice of watermelon (or drawing materials)
steps: Compare an atom to a watermelon where the red part is the positive charge.
Place 'seeds' (beads) to represent electrons studded in the positive sphere.
Discuss why the atom is neutral as a whole.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that in Thomson's model, electrons are embedded in a positively charged sphere.
activities_projects.evaluation: State the two main postulates of Thomson’s model (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Rutherford’s Scattering Analogy
activities_projects.objective: To understand why most alpha particles passed through the gold foil.
steps: Throw stones at a solid wall and listen for the sound.
Throw stones at a barbed-wire fence and observe how many pass through.
Relate the gaps in the fence to the empty space inside an atom.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that most of the space inside an atom is empty.
activities_projects.evaluation: Why did Rutherford select a gold foil for his experiment? (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Bohr’s Energy Levels Sketch
activities_projects.objective: To draw and label the discrete orbits of an atom.
steps: Draw a small center for the nucleus.
Draw four concentric circles around it.
Label the shells as K, L, M, and N or n=1, 2, 3, and 4.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that electrons revolve in specific shells without radiating energy.
activities_projects.evaluation: Draw a sketch of Bohr’s model with three shells (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Sub-atomic Particle Comparison
activities_projects.objective: To identify and compare electrons, protons, and neutrons.
steps: Create cards for Electron (e), Proton (p), and Neutron (n).
Mark their charges: -1, +1, and 0.
Compare their masses: Proton and Neutron are 1 unit each, while Electron is negligible.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies the three fundamental particles that make up an atom.
activities_projects.evaluation: Which sub-atomic particle was discovered by J. Chadwick? (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: The 2n² Rule Practice
activities_projects.objective: To calculate the maximum number of electrons in a shell.
steps: Use the formula 2n² where 'n' is the shell number.
Calculate for K-shell (n=1), L-shell (n=2), and M-shell (n=3).
Verify the results: 2, 8, and 18.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Mastery of the rules for electron distribution in different orbits.
activities_projects.evaluation: What is the maximum number of electrons that can be in the outermost orbit? (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Electronic Configuration Modeling
activities_projects.objective: To display the atomic structure of first eighteen elements.
steps: Choose an element like Sodium (Z=11) or Carbon (Z=6).
Draw the nucleus and shells.
Place 'electrons' (stickers) in shells starting from the inner K-shell.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Visualizes how electrons are distributed in shells like 2, 8, 1 for Sodium.
activities_projects.evaluation: Write the distribution of electrons in a Lithium atom (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Valency Calculator
activities_projects.objective: To determine the combining capacity of elements.
steps: Identify the number of valence electrons (outermost shell).
If valence electrons are ≤ 4, valency = valence electrons (e.g., Magnesium = 2).
If valence electrons are > 4, subtract from 8 (e.g., Fluorine: 8 - 7 = 1).
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands how atoms react to achieve a full octet in the outermost shell.
activities_projects.evaluation: What is the valency of Chlorine? (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Atomic vs. Mass Number
steps: Define Atomic Number (Z) as the number of protons.
Define Mass Number (A) as the sum of protons and neutrons.
Practice the notation ZAX using Nitrogen (714N).
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that mass of an atom resides in the nucleus.
activities_projects.evaluation: If an atom has 8 protons and 8 neutrons, find its Mass Number (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Isotope Matching
activities_projects.objective: To learn about atoms of the same element with different masses.
materials: Diagrams of Protium, Deuterium, and Tritium
steps: Compare the three isotopes of Hydrogen.
Observe that they all have 1 proton but different numbers of neutrons.
Discuss uses of isotopes, like Cobalt-60 for cancer treatment.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that isotopes have the same atomic number but different mass numbers.
activities_projects.evaluation: Name the three isotopes of Hydrogen (10 marks).
